chunk_summary: This section outlines outpatient triage strategies for vegetarian and regional feeding patterns, emphasizing risk classification, growth assessment, symptom triggers, and follow-up intervals for children with dietary risks.
learning_objectives: Understand how to classify vegetarian dietary patterns and their impact on child nutrition.
Learn to assess growth trends and acute risk using weight-for-height, BMI-for-age, and MUAC thresholds.
Identify urgent symptoms linked to dietary deficiencies and assign appropriate follow-up intervals.
Develop structured action plans for children with varying levels of dietary risk.
key_facts: Milk displacement can lead to low iron and protein intake; document daily milk or plant beverage volume explicitly.
Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is suspected with weight-for-height < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm, or bilateral pitting edema.
Vitamin B12 deficiency may present with neuroregression, lethargy, hypotonia, or seizures, especially in vegan diets.
Follow-up intervals: Green (routine intervals), Amber (4–6 weeks), Orange (2–4 weeks based on age).
Targeted labs for Orange risk include CBC, ferritin, vitamin B12 indices, and bone panel for suspected rickets.
reference_quote: Suspect SAM with weight-for-height < -3 SD, MUAC <115 mm, or bilateral pitting edema. (Source: WHO Child Growth Standards)
